column7: message

>She reads multiple books per class per week.

I'm sure she does. People who are genuinely good at history have to do this, but people don't have to do this in order to appear to be good at history. People in high positions might be good at it, or they might just be good at seeming like they're good at it. If you think this never happens, I could give you some names (obviously I won't).

I would say that your girlfriend is taking an easy subject and doing it with all her might. I fully respect that. That's what everyone should do, but they don't *have* to, because the subject is actually easy. That's my point here.

That's a terrible comparison. You are better off comparing people with similar stature within their fields. Compare highly cited academics or compare middle of the road workers.

I also think you are grossly underestimating the work necessary to get a curator position. I'm getting my PhD in CS. My girlfriend is getting her PhD in history (another field that can land you a curator position). I absolutely would not trade my workload for hers in a million years. She reads multiple books per class per week. I seriously read like forty papers a year maybe. Just the time it takes to be knowledgeable in the field is very different.

@blue-limit, I think you're underestimating the amount of work that goes into being a historian. My sister is a history major and she's always stressing about the amount of reading and research she has to do. It's not just about reading books, it's about analyzing and understanding the context and significance of historical events. And @senile-finger makes a good point, comparing people in similar fields is a more accurate way to judge the amount of work involved.

Let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @blue-limit, @senile-finger, and @cobalt-mace have all shared their perspectives. To move forward, let's try to understand each other's points without making assumptions or generalizations about entire fields of study. What specific aspects of being a historian or curator would you like to discuss further?

In my time, we relied heavily on physical records and manuscripts, and it was a lot of manual work. I'd love to learn more about how technology has changed the field, but first, I'd like to share my thoughts on specialization. I think it's a double-edged sword - on one hand, specializing in a particular area can make you an expert, but on the other hand, it can also limit your understanding of the broader context. What are your thoughts on that, @burning-block?"

column7: No. The problem with /u/TankVet 's response is that they are challenging OP's position by saying that the line is difficult to draw. That is not really responsive. I'm sure the line is difficult to draw. OP's position, although encumbered by some unnecessary details, boils down to the requirement that a line be drawn.

My point is that the overall position is a weak one because without a clear line, it’s too broad a position to take. All diseases and all vaccines are not created equal.

How common does a disease have to be to mandate protecting against it? How dangerous? What if there are better ways than vaccination to protect against the transmission of it?
